content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Robin Gray 
Waterwheel Drive, Apt. #8 
Sacramento, California 95833 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-11-089a 
Dear Ms. Gray: 
June 27, 2011 
This letter is in response to your request that we correct a statement in our recitation of 
facts in our previous Advice Letter No. A-11-089, dated May 26, 2011. This response letter, 
which includes the clarified facts, will replace the original letter. 
FACTS 
You are employed by the Department as an Associate Government Program 
Analyst/Ombudsman. In that capacity, you assist mental health consumers with identifying 
mental health resource at the county level. You link mental health consumers to services and 
problem-solve on issues related to quality and access to services. In addition, you respond to 
inquiries of consumers, providers or representatives from county mental health plans. You also 
brief management on issues faced by the consumers and the public mental health system. In this 
capacity, you also write policies and procedures for staff to follow when interfacing with 
consumers. You indicated in our telephone conversation that you do not write proposals for 
information technology bids, your position is not listed in the Department's conflict-of-interest 
code, and that you do not participate in making or influencing governmental decisions. 
In our previous letter, we stated that "your work at the Department involves writing 
requests for offers from vendors relating to programs at the start of a program." In our 
conversation of June 14, you clarified that, in fact, your prior work at the Department involved 
writing requests for offers from vendors relating to programs at the start of a program, but you no 
longer perform this work in your current position. Similarly, in the past you had oversight over 
contracts, but do not do so now. '' 
You would like to work part-time as an independent contractor for a private company to 
write proposals for funding from both state and local government agencies. You state that your 
work for the company would be limited to writing proposals for programs regarding information 


File No. A-l 1-089a 
Page No. 2 
technology, and that the company is contracting with you because of your technical writing 
abilities. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform their 
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the 
financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) Section 87100 
prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her 
official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. 
Because you will have a financial interest in the private business entity if you accept their 
offer of employment, you may not make, participate in the making or use your official position 
to influence a governmental decision that will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial 
effect on the business entity. 
There are, however, other laws implicated in your account of the facts. We urge you to 
consult with Department's management or counsel to determine if your proposed work is 
restricted by the agency's statement of incompatible activities or other laws. 
VJ:jgl 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
John W. Wallace 
Assistant General Counsel 
?IL&d 
By: Valentina Joyce 
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question_synthetic: A Department of Mental Health employee seeks to work part-time as an independent contractor writing grant proposals for a private company and needs to understand what conflicts of interest or restrictions apply under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The employee cannot make, participate in making, or use their official position to influence governmental decisions that would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the private business entity in which they have a financial interest. The FPPC also recommends consulting with the Department's management or counsel to determine if other applicable laws or agency incompatibility policies restrict the proposed work.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: This is a replacement/corrected version of a previous advice letter (A-11-089 from May 26, 2011). The document includes a facts section and analysis rather than traditional question/conclusion sections. The clarification involved removing language about current proposal-writing duties and contract oversight responsibilities that no longer apply in the employee's current position.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: A Department of Mental Health employee seeks to work part-time as an independent contractor writing grant proposals for a private company and needs to understand what conflicts of interest or restrictions apply under the Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION: The employee cannot make, participate in making, or use their official position to influence governmental decisions that would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the private business entity in which they have a financial interest. The FPPC also recommends consulting with the Department's management or counsel to determine if other applicable laws or agency incompatibility policies restrict the proposed work.

embedding.summary: This FPPC advice letter responds to a correction request from a Department employee regarding facts in a previous letter. The employee cannot use their government position to influence decisions affecting a private company they work for as an independent contractor, though they should also consult their agency about other applicable restrictions.
